subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, Freedom House, RSF, HRW, V-Dem, FPRI) + international press (RFE/RL, Eurasianet); state media EXCLUDED on governance.

Central Asia's historically freest state in rapid authoritarian backsliding under President Japarov - a 2021 super-presidential constitution and a 2024-2025 media crackdown (Kloop shut, journalists jailed, 'foreign agents' and media-registration laws).

Standout features: a genuine but fragile boom (~9-11% growth on Russia-bound re-exports, remittances, and Kumtor gold, with sanctions-circumvention and overheating risk), eased Tajikistan-border risk (a March 2025 demarcation treaty), and chronic hydropower-driven winter energy crises.

economic.notes: Strong growth and falling debt, but on a narrow, Russia-linked, overheating-prone base.

fiscal_state.notes: Public debt fell to ~36.6% of GDP (2024) on strong growth and a 2024 surplus, staying under ~42% even as the deficit widens. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: A relatively open managed float with an inflation-targeting central bank, but small, dollarized, and Russia-linked. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The som is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: ~9.4% in 2025, above the 5-7% target, with overheating signs. (IMF; National Statistical Committee)

banking_stability.notes: Rapid credit expansion and re-export-driven inflows raise overheating/AML concerns against contained headline metrics. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: The boom is built on Russia-bound re-exports (sanctions-circumvention exposure) plus gold and remittances - a narrow, externally fragile base. (IMF; Eurasianet)

institutional.notes: Backsliding fast under a super-presidential constitution, though still the region's most open.

rule_of_law.notes: Backsliding under the 2021 super-presidential constitution, with politicized prosecutions, though still less total than its neighbours. (Freedom House; HRW 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Comparatively open economic reporting (a normal IMF Art IV cycle) versus rising political opacity. (IMF; V-Dem)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Functional but patronage-driven and destabilized by recurrent purges (the 2026 dismantling of Tashiyev's network). (FPRI 2026; Eurasianet)

press_freedom.notes: RSF 146/180 (2026, still the region's highest) but falling - Kloop shut, journalists jailed, and new 'foreign agents'/registration laws. (RSF 2026; HRW 2025)

political_social.notes: Consolidated power but high base-rate instability, with shrinking-but-contested civic space.

political_stability.notes: Japarov consolidated power and won the 2026 tandem rupture, but a history of three revolutions and ongoing purges keep base-rate instability high. (FPRI 2026; Eurasianet)

civil_liberties.notes: Shrinking but still the region's most contested civic space, with protest increasingly criminalized. (Freedom House; HRW 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: More open and historically migration-tolerant than its neighbours, but not a secure long-term destination amid rising nationalism. (Freedom House)

social_cohesion.notes: North-south and Uzbek-minority cleavages persist; the 2025 border deal eased the external flashpoint with Tajikistan. (HRW; ICG)

demographics.notes: A young, growing population, though heavily dependent on labour emigration to Russia. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Russia-dependent with a China hedge, eased border risk, but sanctions-circumvention exposure.

alliance_reliability.notes: A CSTO/EAEU member deeply dependent on Russia (remittances, trade, security) with a Chinese-investment hedge - a follower, not autonomous. (IMF; ICG)

conflict_involvement.notes: The March 2025 border treaty with Tajikistan reopened crossings and sharply cut the 2021-22 clash risk, with residual caution warranted. (HRW; Euronews)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Not sanctioned but a documented Russia sanctions-circumvention hub, creating secondary-sanctions and banking-access risk. (IMF; Eurasianet)

physical_practical.notes: Glacier-melt-driven winter energy crises and high seismic/landslide hazard.

climate.notes: Severe glacier melt and falling run-off - the Toktogul reservoir lost ~20% of its water in a year, driving winter energy crises. (Al-Monitor; IEA)

healthcare.notes: Basic public coverage with uneven rural access and heavy reliance on remittance-funded private care. (WHO; World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: ~95% hydro-dependent electricity makes winter shortages chronic, reliant on Kazakh/Uzbek emergency imports in 2025-26. (IEA; Asia-Plus)

crime_safety.notes: Moderate street crime and periodic unrest/protest risk in Bishkek and Osh. (soft/indirect)

disaster_insurance.notes: High seismic and glacial-lake-outburst/landslide hazard with very low insurance penetration. (seismic-hazard literature)

summary: Central Asia's historically freest state now sliding toward its neighbours' norm: President Japarov's 2021 super-presidential constitution and a sustained 2024-2025 crackdown (Kloop shut, journalists jailed, 'foreign-agents' and media-registration laws) have gutted what was a regional press-freedom haven, even as it still tops the region on RSF. The economy is booming (~9-11% growth) but on fragile pillars - Russia-bound re-exports, remittances, and Kumtor gold - that carry sanctions-circumvention and overheating risk. A landmark March 2025 border-demarcation treaty with Tajikistan defused the deadliest external flashpoint, but the 2026 collapse of the Japarov-Tashiyev tandem and a history of three revolutions keep elite instability elevated, compounded by chronic hydropower-driven winter energy crises.
